claims.text: Humans have evolved a faculty of reason that is often deployed to argue against reasoning itself. This creates a paradox where reasonableness and correctness diverge: something can be logically sound yet factually wrong, or logically weak yet fundamentally correct.
claims.speaker: Unknown
claims.mechanism: The faculty evolved to rationalize decisions post-hoc rather than to discover truth; reasonableness becomes a rhetorical tool.

claims.text: In business cultures that value reason above all else, it is extremely difficult to communicate that the persuasiveness of a rationale is a poor measure of whether a decision is actually correct.
claims.speaker: Unknown
conditions: audience values logical argumentation as primary decision criterion
claims.mechanism: Reason-first cultures treat compelling arguments as proxies for truth, creating resistance to decisions grounded in non-rational constraints or evidence.
bounded_by: Does not apply to purely mathematical or empirical decisions where logic directly maps to outcome

practices.text: When evaluating decisions, separate the quality of the reasoning from the quality of the outcome; do not use the persuasiveness of an argument as the primary criterion for judgment.
